sentences.text: This chapter describes the machine-level operations available in machine-mode (M-mode), which is the highest privilege mode in a RISC-V hart. M-mode is used for low-level access to a hardware platform and is the first mode entered at reset. M-mode can also be used to implement features that are too difficult or expensive to implement in hardware directly.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13

sentences.text: The RISC-V machine-level ISA contains a common core that is extended depending on which other privilege levels are supported and other details of the hardware implementation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13

sentences.text: In addition to the machine-level CSRs described in this section, M-mode code can access all CSRs at lower privilege levels.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs

sentences.text: The `misa` CSR is a *WARL* read-write register reporting the ISA supported by the hart.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: This register must be readable in any implementation, but a value of zero can be returned to indicate the `misa` register has not been implemented, requiring that CPU capabilities be determined through a separate non-standard mechanism.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: .Machine ISA register (misa) include::images/bytefield/misareg.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: The MXL (Machine XLEN) field encodes the native base integer ISA width as shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: The MXL field is read-only.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: If `misa` is nonzero, the MXL field indicates the effective XLEN in M-mode, a constant termed MXLEN. XLEN is never greater than MXLEN, but XLEN might be smaller than MXLEN in less-privileged modes.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: .Encoding of MXL field in `misa` | |MXL |XLEN |1 + 2 + 3 |32 + 64 + Reserved |
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: The `misa` CSR is MXLEN bits wide.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: The Extensions field encodes the presence of the standard extensions, with a single bit per letter of the alphabet (bit 0 encodes presence of extension "A" , bit 1 encodes presence of extension "B", through to bit 25 which encodes "Z").
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: The "I" bit will be set for the RV32I and RV64I base ISAs, and the "E" bit will be set for RV32E and RV64E.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: The Extensions field is a *WARL* field that can contain writable bits where the implementation allows the supported ISA to be modified.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: At reset, the Extensions field shall contain the maximal set of supported extensions, and "I" shall be selected over "E" if both are available.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When a standard extension is disabled by clearing its bit in `misa`, the instructions and CSRs defined or modified by the extension revert to their defined or reserved behaviors as if the extension is not implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: All bits that are reserved for future use must return zero when read.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: .Encoding of Extensions field in `misa`. | |Bit |Character |Description |0 |A |Atomic extension |1 |B |B extension |2 |C |Compressed extension |3 |D |Double-precision floating-point extension |4 |E |RV32E/64E base ISA |5 |F |Single-precision floating-point extension |6 |G |Reserved |7 |H |Hypervisor extension |8 |I |RV32I/64I base ISA |9 |J |Reserved |10 |K |Reserved |11 |L |Reserved |12 |M |Integer Multiply/Divide extension |13 |N |Tentatively reserved for User-Level Interrupts extension |14 |O |Reserved |15 |P |Tentatively reserved for Packed-SIMD extension |16 |Q |Quad-precision floating-point extension |17 |R |Reserved |18 |S |Supervisor mode implemented |19 |T |Reserved |20 |U |User mode implemented |21 |V |Vector extension |22 |W |Reserved |23 |X |Non-standard extensions implemented |24 |Y |Reserved |25 |Z |Reserved |
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: The "X" bit will be set if there are any non-standard extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When the "B" bit is 1, the implementation supports the instructions provided by the Zba, Zbb, and Zbs extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When the "B" bit is 0, it indicates that the implementation might not support one or more of the Zba, Zbb, or Zbs extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When the "M" bit is 1, the implementation supports all multiply and division instructions defined by the M extension.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When the "M" bit is 0, it indicates that the implementation might not support those instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: However if the Zmmul extension is supported then the multiply instructions it specifies are supported irrespective of the value of the "M" bit.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When the "S" bit is 1, the implementation supports supervisor mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When the "S" bit is 0, the implementation might not support supervisor mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When the "U" bit is 1, the implementation supports user mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When the "U" bit is 0, the implementation might not support user mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: Unless `misa` is all read-only zero, the "E" bit always reads as the complement of the "I" bit.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: If an execution environment supports both RV32E and RV32I, software can select RV32E by clearing the "I" bit.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: If an ISA feature x depends on an ISA feature y, then attempting to enable feature x but disable feature y results in both features being disabled.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: For example, setting "F"=0 and "D"=1 results in both "F" and "D" being cleared.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: Similarly, setting "U"=0 and "S"=1" results in both "U" and "S" being cleared.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: An implementation may impose additional constraints on the collective setting of two or more `misa` fields, in which case they function collectively as a single *WARL* field.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: An attempt to write an unsupported combination causes those bits to be set to some supported combination.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: Writing `misa` may increase IALIGN, e.g., by disabling the "C" extension.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: If an instruction that would write `misa` increases IALIGN, and the subsequent instruction's address is not IALIGN-bit aligned, the write to `misa` is suppressed, leaving `misa` unchanged.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: When software enables an extension that was previously disabled, then all state uniquely associated with that extension is UNSPECIFIED, unless otherwise specified by that extension.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: NOTE: Although one of the bits 25--0 in `misa` being set to 1 implies that the corresponding feature is implemented, the inverse is not necessarily true: one of these bits being clear does not necessarily imply that the corresponding feature is not implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: This follows from the fact that, when a feature is not implemented, the corresponding opcodes and CSRs become reserved, not necessarily illegal.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine ISA (`misa`) Register

sentences.text: The `mvendorid` CSR is a 32-bit read-only register providing the JEDEC manufacturer ID of the provider of the core.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Vendor ID (`mvendorid`) Register

sentences.text: This register must be readable in any implementation, but a value of 0 can be returned to indicate the field is not implemented or that this is a non-commercial implementation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Vendor ID (`mvendorid`) Register

sentences.text: .Vendor ID register (`mvendorid`) include::images/bytefield/mvendorid.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Vendor ID (`mvendorid`) Register

sentences.text: JEDEC manufacturer IDs are ordinarily encoded as a sequence of one-byte continuation codes `0x7f`, terminated by a one-byte ID not equal to `0x7f`, with an odd parity bit in the most-significant bit of each byte. `mvendorid` encodes the number of one-byte continuation codes in the Bank field, and encodes the final byte in the Offset field, discarding the parity bit.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Vendor ID (`mvendorid`) Register

sentences.text: For example, the JEDEC manufacturer ID `0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x7f 0x8a` (twelve continuation codes followed by `0x8a`) would be encoded in the `mvendorid` CSR as `0x60a`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Vendor ID (`mvendorid`) Register

sentences.text: The `marchid` CSR is an MXLEN-bit read-only register encoding the base microarchitecture of the hart.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Architecture ID (`marchid`) Register

sentences.text: This register must be readable in any implementation, but a value of 0 can be returned to indicate the field is not implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Architecture ID (`marchid`) Register

sentences.text: The combination of `mvendorid` and `marchid` should uniquely identify the type of hart microarchitecture that is implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Architecture ID (`marchid`) Register

sentences.text: .Machine Architecture ID (`marchid`) register include::images/bytefield/marchid.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Architecture ID (`marchid`) Register

sentences.text: Open-source project architecture IDs are allocated globally by RISC-V International, and have non-zero architecture IDs with a zero most-significant-bit (MSB).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Architecture ID (`marchid`) Register

sentences.text: Commercial architecture IDs are allocated by each commercial vendor independently, but must have the MSB set and cannot contain zero in the remaining MXLEN-1 bits.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Architecture ID (`marchid`) Register

sentences.text: The `mimpid` CSR provides a unique encoding of the version of the processor implementation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Implementation ID (`mimpid`) Register

sentences.text: This register must be readable in any implementation, but a value of 0 can be returned to indicate that the field is not implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Implementation ID (`mimpid`) Register

sentences.text: The Implementation value should reflect the design of the RISC-V processor itself and not any surrounding system.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Implementation ID (`mimpid`) Register

sentences.text: .Machine Implementation ID (`mimpid`) register include::images/bytefield/mimpid.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Implementation ID (`mimpid`) Register

sentences.text: The `mhartid` CSR is an MXLEN-bit read-only register containing the integer ID of the hardware thread running the code.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Hart ID (`mhartid`) Register

sentences.text: This register must be readable in any implementation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Hart ID (`mhartid`) Register

sentences.text: Hart IDs might not necessarily be numbered contiguously in a multiprocessor system, but one hart must have a hart ID of zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Hart ID (`mhartid`) Register

sentences.text: Hart IDs must be unique within the execution environment.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Hart ID (`mhartid`) Register

sentences.text: .Hart ID (`mhartid`) register include::images/bytefield/mhartid.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Hart ID (`mhartid`) Register

sentences.text: The `mstatus` register is an MXLEN-bit read/write register formatted as shown in for RV32 and for RV64.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers

sentences.text: The `mstatus` register keeps track of and controls the hart’s current operating state. A restricted view of `mstatus` appears as the `sstatus` register in the S-level ISA.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers

sentences.text: .Machine-mode status (`mstatus`) register for RV32 include::images/wavedrom/mstatusreg-rv321.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers

sentences.text: .Machine-mode status (`mstatus`) register for RV64 include::images/wavedrom/mstatusreg.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers

sentences.text: For RV32 only, `mstatush` is a 32-bit read/write register formatted as shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers

sentences.text: Bits 30:4 of `mstatush` generally contain the same fields found in bits 62:36 of `mstatus` for RV64.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers

sentences.text: Fields SD, SXL, and UXL do not exist in `mstatush`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers

sentences.text: .Additional machine-mode status (`mstatush`) register for RV32. include::images/wavedrom/mstatushreg.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers

sentences.text: Global interrupt-enable bits, MIE and SIE, are provided for M-mode and S-mode respectively.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: These bits are primarily used to guarantee atomicity with respect to interrupt handlers in the current privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: When a hart is executing in privilege mode x, interrupts are globally enabled when xIE=1 and globally disabled when xIE=0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: Interrupts for lower-privilege modes, w<x, are always globally disabled regardless of the setting of any global wIE bit for the lower-privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: Interrupts for higher-privilege modes, y>x, are always globally enabled regardless of the setting of the global yIE bit for the higher-privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: Higher-privilege-level code can use separate per-interrupt enable bits to disable selected higher-privilege-mode interrupts before ceding control to a lower-privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: If supervisor mode is not implemented, then SIE and SPIE are read-only 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: To support nested traps, each privilege mode x that can respond to interrupts has a two-level stack of interrupt-enable bits and privilege modes. xPIE holds the value of the interrupt-enable bit active prior to the trap, and xPP holds the previous privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: The xPP fields can only hold privilege modes up to x, so MPP is two bits wide and SPP is one bit wide.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: When a trap is taken from privilege mode y into privilege mode x, xPIE is set to the value of xIE; xIE is set to 0; and xPP is set to y.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: An MRET or SRET instruction is used to return from a trap in M-mode or S-mode respectively.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: When executing an xRET instruction, supposing xPP holds the value y, xIE is set to xPIE; the privilege mode is changed to y; xPIE is set to 1; and xPP is set to the least-privileged supported mode (U if U-mode is implemented, else M).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: If y{ne}M, xRET also sets MPRV=0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: xPP fields are *WARL* fields that can hold only privilege mode x and any implemented privilege mode lower than x.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: If privilege mode x is not implemented, then xPP must be read-only 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Privilege and Global Interrupt-Enable Stack in `mstatus` register

sentences.text: A double trap typically arises during a sensitive phase in trap handling operations -- when an exception or interrupt occurs while the trap handler (the component responsible for managing these events) is in a non-reentrant state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: This non-reentrancy usually occurs in the early phase of trap handling, wherein the trap handler has not yet preserved the necessary state to handle and resume from the trap.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The occurrence of a trap during this phase can lead to an overwrite of critical state information, resulting in the loss of data needed to recover from the initial trap.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The trap that caused this critical error condition is henceforth called the unexpected trap.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Trap handlers are designed to neither enable interrupts nor cause exceptions during this phase of handling.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: However, managing Hardware-Error exceptions, which may occur unpredictably, presents significant challenges in trap handler implementation due to the potential risk of a double trap.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The M-mode-disable-trap (`MDT`) bit is a WARL field introduced by the Smdbltrp extension.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Upon reset, the `MDT` field is set to 1.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When the `MDT` bit is set to 1 by an explicit CSR write, the `MIE` (Machine Interrupt Enable) bit is cleared to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: For RV64, this clearing occurs regardless of the value written, if any, to the `MIE` bit by the same write.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The `MIE` bit can only be set to 1 by an explicit CSR write if the `MDT` bit is already 0 or, for RV64, is being set to 0 by the same write (For RV32, the `MDT` bit is in `mstatush` and the `MIE` bit in `mstatus` register).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When a trap is to be taken into M-mode, if the `MDT` bit is currently 0, it is then set to 1, and the trap is delivered as expected.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: However, if `MDT` is already set to 1, then this is an unexpected trap.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When the Smrnmi extension is implemented, a trap caused by an RNMI is not considered an unexpected trap irrespective of the state of the `MDT` bit. A trap caused by an RNMI does not set the `MDT` bit.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: However, a trap that occurs when executing in M-mode with `mnstatus.NMIE` set to 0 is an unexpected trap.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: In the event of a unexpected trap, the handling is as follows:
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When the Smrnmi extension is implemented and `mnstatus.NMIE` is 1, the hart traps to the RNMI handler.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: To deliver this trap, the `mnepc` and `mncause` registers are written with the values that the unexpected trap would have written to the `mepc` and `mcause` registers respectively.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The privilege mode information fields in the `mnstatus` register are written to indicate M-mode and its `NMIE` field is set to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When the Smrnmi extension is not implemented, or if the Smrnmi extension is implemented and `mnstatus.NMIE` is 0, the hart enters a critical-error state without updating any architectural state, including the `pc`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: This state involves ceasing execution, disabling all interrupts (including NMIs), and asserting a `critical-error` signal to the platform.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Whether performance counters and timers are updated in the critical-error state is UNSPECIFIED.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When the Smdbltrp extension is implemented, executing an `MRET` instruction, or executing an `SRET` instruction while the current privilege mode is M, has the following additional effects.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The `MDT` bit is set to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If the Ssdbltrp extension is also implemented, and the new privilege mode is U, VS, or VU, then `sstatus.SDT` is also set to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Additionally, if it is VU, then `vsstatus.SDT` is also set to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When the Smdbltrp extension is implemented, the `MNRET` instruction, provided by the Smrnmi extension, sets the `MDT` bit to 0 if the new privilege mode is not M.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If the Ssdbltrp extension is also implemented, and the new privilege mode is U, VS, or VU, then `sstatus.SDT` is also set to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Additionally, if it is VU, then `vsstatus.SDT` is also set to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Double Trap Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: For RV64 harts, the SXL and UXL fields are *WARL* fields that control the value of XLEN for S-mode and U-mode, respectively.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The encoding of these fields is the same as the MXL field of `misa`, shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The effective XLEN in S-mode and U-mode are termed SXLEN and UXLEN, respectively.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When MXLEN=32, the SXL and UXL fields do not exist, and SXLEN=32 and UXLEN=32.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When MXLEN=64, if S-mode is not supported, then SXL is read-only zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Otherwise, it is a *WARL* field that encodes the current value of SXLEN.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: In particular, an implementation may make SXL be a read-only field whose value always ensures that SXLEN=MXLEN.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When MXLEN=64, if U-mode is not supported, then UXL is read-only zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Otherwise, it is a *WARL* field that encodes the current value of UXLEN.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: In particular, an implementation may make UXL be a read-only field whose value always ensures that UXLEN=MXLEN or UXLEN=SXLEN.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If S-mode is implemented, the set of legal values that the UXL field may assume excludes those that would cause UXLEN to be greater than SXLEN.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Whenever XLEN in any mode is set to a value less than the widest supported XLEN, all operations must ignore source operand register bits above the configured XLEN, and must sign-extend results to fill the entire widest supported XLEN in the destination register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Similarly, `pc` bits above XLEN are ignored, and when the `pc` is written, it is sign-extended to fill the widest supported XLEN.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Some HINT instructions are encoded as integer computational instructions that overwrite their destination register with its current value, e.g., `c.addi x8, 0`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When such a HINT is executed with XLEN < MXLEN and bits MXLEN..XLEN of the destination register not all equal to bit XLEN-1, it is implementation-defined whether bits MXLEN..XLEN of the destination register are unchanged or are overwritten with copies of bit XLEN-1.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: NOTE: This definition allows implementations to elide register write-back for some HINTs, while allowing them to execute other HINTs in the same manner as other integer computational instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The implementation choice is observable only by privilege modes with an XLEN setting greater than the current XLEN; it is invisible to the current privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Base ISA Control in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The MPRV (Modify PRiVilege) bit modifies the effective privilege mode, i.e., the privilege level at which explicit memory accesses execute.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When MPRV=0, explicit memory accesses behave as normal, using the translation and protection mechanisms of the current privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When MPRV=1, load and store memory addresses are translated and protected, and endianness is applied, as though the current privilege mode were set to MPP.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Instruction address-translation and protection are unaffected by the setting of MPRV. MPRV is read-only 0 if U-mode is not supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: An MRET or SRET instruction that changes the privilege mode to a mode less privileged than M also sets MPRV=0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The MXR (Make eXecutable Readable) bit modifies the privilege with which loads access virtual memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When MXR=0, only loads from pages marked readable (R=1 in ) will succeed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When MXR=1, loads from pages marked either readable or executable (R=1 or X=1) will succeed. MXR has no effect when page-based virtual memory is not in effect. MXR is read-only 0 if S-mode is not supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The SUM (permit Supervisor User Memory access) bit modifies the privilege with which S-mode loads and stores access virtual memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When SUM=0, S-mode memory accesses to pages that are accessible by U-mode (U=1 in ) will fault.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When SUM=1, these accesses are permitted. SUM has no effect when page-based virtual memory is not in effect.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Note that, while SUM is ordinarily ignored when not executing in S-mode, it is in effect when MPRV=1 and MPP=S. SUM is read-only 0 if S-mode is not supported or if `satp`.MODE is read-only 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The MXR and SUM mechanisms only affect the interpretation of permissions encoded in page-table entries.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: In particular, they have no impact on whether access-fault exceptions are raised due to PMAs or PMP.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Memory Privilege in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The MBE, SBE, and UBE bits in `mstatus` and `mstatush` are *WARL* fields that control the endianness of memory accesses other than instruction fetches.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: Instruction fetches are always little-endian.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: MBE controls whether non-instruction-fetch memory accesses made from M-mode (assuming `mstatus`.MPRV=0) are little-endian (MBE=0) or big-endian (MBE=1).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: If S-mode is not supported, SBE is read-only 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: Otherwise, SBE controls whether explicit load and store memory accesses made from S-mode are little-endian (SBE=0) or big-endian (SBE=1).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: If U-mode is not supported, UBE is read-only 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: Otherwise, UBE controls whether explicit load and store memory accesses made from U-mode are little-endian (UBE=0) or big-endian (UBE=1).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: For implicit accesses to supervisor-level memory management data structures, such as page tables, endianness is always controlled by SBE.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: Since changing SBE alters the implementation’s interpretation of these data structures, if any such data structures remain in use across a change to SBE, M-mode software must follow such a change to SBE by executing an SFENCE.VMA instruction with rs1=`x0` and rs2=`x0`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: If S-mode is supported, an implementation may make SBE be a read-only copy of MBE.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: If U-mode is supported, an implementation may make UBE be a read-only copy of either MBE or SBE.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Endianness Control in `mstatus` and `mstatush` Registers

sentences.text: The TVM (Trap Virtual Memory) bit is a *WARL* field that supports intercepting supervisor virtual-memory management operations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When TVM=1, attempts to read or write the `satp` CSR or execute an SFENCE.VMA or SINVAL.VMA instruction while executing in S-mode will raise an illegal-instruction exception.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When TVM=0, these operations are permitted in S-mode. TVM is read-only 0 when S-mode is not supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The TW (Timeout Wait) bit is a *WARL* field that supports intercepting the WFI instruction (see ).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When TW=0, the WFI instruction may execute in modes less privileged than M when not prevented for some other reason.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When TW=1, then if WFI is executed in any less-privileged mode, and it does not complete within an implementation-specific, bounded time limit, the WFI instruction causes an illegal-instruction exception.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: An implementation may have WFI always raise an illegal-instruction exception in modes less privileged than M when TW=1, even if there are pending globally-disabled interrupts when the instruction is executed. TW is read-only 0 when there are no modes less privileged than M.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When S-mode is implemented, then executing WFI in U-mode causes an illegal-instruction exception, regardless of the value of the TW bit, unless the instruction completes within an implementation-specific, bounded time limit.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The TSR (Trap SRET) bit is a *WARL* field that supports intercepting the supervisor exception return instruction, SRET.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When TSR=1, attempts to execute SRET while executing in S-mode will raise an illegal-instruction exception.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When TSR=0, this operation is permitted in S-mode. TSR is read-only 0 when S-mode is not supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Virtualization Support in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Supporting substantial extensions is one of the primary goals of RISC-V, and hence we define a standard interface to allow unchanged privileged-mode code, particularly a supervisor-level OS, to support arbitrary user-mode state extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The FS[1:0] and VS[1:0] *WARL* fields and the XS[1:0] read-only field are used to reduce the cost of context save and restore by setting and tracking the current state of the floating-point unit and any other user-mode extensions respectively.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The FS field encodes the status of the floating-point unit state, including the floating-point registers `f0`–`f31` and the CSRs `fcsr`, `frm`, and `fflags`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The VS field encodes the status of the vector extension state, including the vector registers `v0`–`v31` and the CSRs `vcsr`, `vxrm`, `vxsat`, `vstart`, `vl`, `vtype`, and `vlenb`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The XS field encodes the status of additional user-mode extensions and associated state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: These fields can be checked by a context switch routine to quickly determine whether a state save or restore is required.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If a save or restore is required, additional instructions and CSRs are typically required to effect and optimize the process.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The FS, VS, and XS fields use the same status encoding as shown in , with the four possible status values being Off, Initial, Clean, and Dirty.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: .Encoding of FS[1:0], VS[1:0], and XS[1:0] status fields | |Status |FS and VS Meaning |XS Meaning |0 + 1 + 2 + 3 |Off + Initial + Clean + Dirty |All off + None dirty or clean, some on + None dirty, some clean + Some dirty |
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If the F extension is implemented, the FS field shall not be read-only zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If neither the F extension nor S-mode is implemented, then FS is read-only zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If S-mode is implemented but the F extension is not, FS may optionally be read-only zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If the `v` registers are implemented, the VS field shall not be read-only zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If neither the `v` registers nor S-mode is implemented, then VS is read-only zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If S-mode is implemented but the `v` registers are not, VS may optionally be read-only zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: In harts without additional user extensions requiring new state, the XS field is read-only zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Every additional extension with state provides a CSR field that encodes the equivalent of the XS states.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The XS field represents a summary of all extensions' status as shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The SD bit is a read-only bit that summarizes whether either the FS, VS, or XS fields signal the presence of some dirty state that will require saving extended user context to memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If FS, XS, and VS are all read-only zero, then SD is also always zero.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When an extension's status is set to Off, any instruction that attempts to read or write the corresponding state will cause an illegal-instruction exception.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When the status is Initial, the corresponding state should have an initial constant value.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When the status is Clean, the corresponding state is potentially different from the initial value, but matches the last value stored on a context swap.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When the status is Dirty, the corresponding state has potentially been modified since the last context save.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: During a context save, the responsible privileged code need only write out the corresponding state if its status is Dirty, and can then reset the extension's status to Clean.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: During a context restore, the context need only be loaded from memory if the status is Clean (it should never be Dirty at restore).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If the status is Initial, the context must be set to an initial constant value on context restore to avoid a security hole, but this can be done without accessing memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: For example, the floating-point registers can all be initialized to the immediate value 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The FS and XS fields are read by the privileged code before saving the context.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The FS field is set directly by privileged code when resuming a user context, while the XS field is set indirectly by writing to the status register of the individual extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The status fields will also be updated during execution of instructions, regardless of privilege mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Extensions to the user-mode ISA often include additional user-mode state, and this state can be considerably larger than the base integer registers.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The extensions might only be used for some applications, or might only be needed for short phases within a single application.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: To improve performance, the user-mode extension can define additional instructions to allow user-mode software to return the unit to an initial state or even to turn off the unit.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: For example, a coprocessor might require to be configured before use and can be "unconfigured" after use.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: The unconfigured state would be represented as the Initial state for context save.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If the same application remains running between the unconfigure and the next configure (which would set status to Dirty), there is no need to actually reinitialize the state at the unconfigure instruction, as all state is local to the user process, i.e., the Initial state may only cause the coprocessor state to be initialized to a constant value at context restore, not at every unconfigure.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Executing a user-mode instruction to disable a unit and place it into the Off state will cause an illegal-instruction exception to be raised if any subsequent instruction tries to use the unit before it is turned back on. A user-mode instruction to turn a unit on must also ensure the unit's state is properly initialized, as the unit might have been used by another context meantime.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Changing the setting of FS has no effect on the contents of the floating-point register state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: In particular, setting FS=Off does not destroy the state, nor does setting FS=Initial clear the contents.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Similarly, the setting of VS has no effect on the contents of the vector register state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Other extensions, however, might not preserve state when set to Off.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Implementations may choose to track the dirtiness of the floating-point register state imprecisely by reporting the state to be dirty even when it has not been modified.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: On some implementations, some instructions that do not mutate the floating-point state may cause the state to transition from Initial or Clean to Dirty.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: On other implementations, dirtiness might not be tracked at all, in which case the valid FS states are Off and Dirty, and an attempt to set FS to Initial or Clean causes it to be set to Dirty.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: If an instruction explicitly or implicitly writes a floating-point register or the `fcsr` but does not alter its contents, and FS=Initial or FS=Clean, it is implementation-defined whether FS transitions to Dirty.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Implementations may choose to track the dirtiness of the vector register state in an analogous imprecise fashion, including possibly setting VS to Dirty when software attempts to set VS=Initial or VS=Clean.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: When VS=Initial or VS=Clean, it is implementation-defined whether an instruction that writes a vector register or vector CSR but does not alter its contents causes VS to transition to Dirty.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: shows all the possible state transitions for the FS, VS, or XS status bits.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register

sentences.text: Note that the standard floating-point and vector extensions do not support user-mode unconfigure or disable/enable instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Status (`mstatus` and `mstatush`) Registers > Extension Context Status in `mstatus` Register
