sentences.text: When `MLPE` field is 1, Zicfilp extension is enabled in M-mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Security Configuration (`mseccfg`) Register

sentences.text: When the `MLPE` field is 0, the Zicfilp extension is not enabled in M-mode and the following rules apply to M-mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Security Configuration (`mseccfg`) Register

sentences.text: * The hart does not update the `ELP` state; it remains as `NOLPEXPECTED`. * The `LPAD` instruction operates as a no-op.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Security Configuration (`mseccfg`) Register

sentences.text: When XLEN=32 only, `mseccfgh` is a 32-bit read/write register that aliases bits 63:32 of `mseccfg`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Security Configuration (`mseccfg`) Register

sentences.text: Register `mseccfgh` exists when XLEN=32 and `mseccfg` is implemented; it does not exist when XLEN=64.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level CSRs > Machine Security Configuration (`mseccfg`) Register

sentences.text: Platforms provide a real-time counter, exposed as a memory-mapped machine-mode read-write register, `mtime`. `mtime` must increment at constant frequency, and the platform must provide a mechanism for determining the period of an `mtime` tick.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level Memory-Mapped Registers > Machine Timer (`mtime` and `mtimecmp`) Registers

sentences.text: The `mtime` register will wrap around if the count overflows.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level Memory-Mapped Registers > Machine Timer (`mtime` and `mtimecmp`) Registers

sentences.text: The `mtime` register has a 64-bit precision on all RV32 and RV64 systems.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level Memory-Mapped Registers > Machine Timer (`mtime` and `mtimecmp`) Registers

sentences.text: Platforms provide a 64-bit memory-mapped machine-mode timer compare register (`mtimecmp`). A machine timer interrupt becomes pending whenever `mtime` contains a value greater than or equal to `mtimecmp`, treating the values as unsigned integers.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level Memory-Mapped Registers > Machine Timer (`mtime` and `mtimecmp`) Registers

sentences.text: The interrupt remains posted until `mtimecmp` becomes greater than `mtime` (typically as a result of writing `mtimecmp`).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level Memory-Mapped Registers > Machine Timer (`mtime` and `mtimecmp`) Registers

sentences.text: The interrupt will only be taken if interrupts are enabled and the MTIE bit is set in the `mie` register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Level Memory-Mapped Registers > Machine Timer (`mtime` and `mtimecmp`) Registers

sentences.text: The ECALL instruction is used to make a request to the supporting execution environment.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Environment Call and Breakpoint

sentences.text: When executed in U-mode, S-mode, or M-mode, it generates an environment-call-from-U-mode exception, environment-call-from-S-mode exception, or environment-call-from-M-mode exception, respectively, and performs no other operation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Environment Call and Breakpoint

sentences.text: The EBREAK instruction is used by debuggers to cause control to be transferred back to a debugging environment.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Environment Call and Breakpoint

sentences.text: Unless overridden by an external debug environment, EBREAK raises a breakpoint exception and performs no other operation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Environment Call and Breakpoint

sentences.text: ECALL and EBREAK cause the receiving privilege mode’s `epc` register to be set to the address of the ECALL or EBREAK instruction itself, not the address of the following instruction.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Environment Call and Breakpoint

sentences.text: As ECALL and EBREAK cause synchronous exceptions, they are not considered to retire, and should not increment the `minstret` CSR.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Environment Call and Breakpoint

sentences.text: Instructions to return from trap are encoded under the PRIV minor opcode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Trap-Return Instructions

sentences.text: include::images/wavedrom/trap-return.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Trap-Return Instructions

sentences.text: To return after handling a trap, there are separate trap return instructions per privilege level, MRET and SRET. MRET is always provided. SRET must be provided if supervisor mode is supported, and should raise an illegal-instruction exception otherwise. SRET should also raise an illegal-instruction exception when TSR=1 in `mstatus`, as described in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Trap-Return Instructions

sentences.text: An xRET instruction can be executed in privilege mode x or higher, where executing a lower-privilege xRET instruction will pop the relevant lower-privilege interrupt enable and privilege mode stack.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Trap-Return Instructions

sentences.text: Attempting to execute an xRET instruction in a mode less privileged than x will raise an illegal-instruction exception.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Trap-Return Instructions

sentences.text: In addition to manipulating the privilege stack as described in , xRET sets the `pc` to the value stored in the `xepc` register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Trap-Return Instructions

sentences.text: If the Zalrsc extension is supported, the xRET instruction is allowed to clear any outstanding LR address reservation but is not required to.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Trap-Return Instructions

sentences.text: Trap handlers should explicitly clear the reservation if required (e.g., by using a dummy SC) before executing the xRET.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Trap-Return Instructions

sentences.text: The Wait for Interrupt instruction (WFI) informs the implementation that the current hart can be stalled until an interrupt might need servicing.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: Execution of the WFI instruction can also be used to inform the hardware platform that suitable interrupts should preferentially be routed to this hart. WFI is available in all privileged modes, and optionally available to U-mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: This instruction may raise an illegal-instruction exception when TW=1 in `mstatus`, as described in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: include::images/wavedrom/wfi.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: If an enabled interrupt is present or later becomes present while the hart is stalled, the interrupt trap will be taken on the following instruction, i.e., execution resumes in the trap handler and `mepc` = `pc` + 4.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: Implementations are permitted to resume execution for any reason, even if an enabled interrupt has not become pending.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: Hence, a legal implementation is to simply implement the WFI instruction as a NOP.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: The WFI instruction can also be executed when interrupts are disabled.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: The operation of WFI must be unaffected by the global interrupt bits in `mstatus` (MIE and SIE) and the delegation register `mideleg` (i.e., the hart must resume if a locally enabled interrupt becomes pending, even if it has been delegated to a less-privileged mode), but should honor the individual interrupt enables (e.g, MTIE) (i.e., implementations should avoid resuming the hart if the interrupt is pending but not individually enabled). WFI is also required to resume execution for locally enabled interrupts pending at any privilege level, regardless of the global interrupt enable at each privilege level.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: If the event that causes the hart to resume execution does not cause an interrupt to be taken, execution will resume at `pc` + 4, and software must determine what action to take, including looping back to repeat the WFI if there was no actionable event.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Wait for Interrupt

sentences.text: The subspace of the SYSTEM major opcode shown in is designated for custom use.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Custom SYSTEM Instructions

sentences.text: It is recommended that these instructions use bits 29:28 to designate the minimum required privilege mode, as do other SYSTEM instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Custom SYSTEM Instructions

sentences.text: .SYSTEM instruction encodings designated for custom use. include::images/bytefield/cust-sys-instr.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Machine-Mode Privileged Instructions > Custom SYSTEM Instructions

sentences.text: Upon reset, a hart’s privilege mode is set to M.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: The `mstatus` fields MIE and MPRV are reset to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: If little-endian memory accesses are supported, the `mstatus`/`mstatush` field MBE is reset to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: The `misa` register is reset to enable the maximal set of supported extensions, as described in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: For implementations with the Zalrsc standard extension, there is no valid load reservation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: The `pc` is set to an implementation-defined reset vector.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: The `mcause` register is set to a value indicating the cause of the reset.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: Writable PMP registers’ A and L fields are set to 0, unless the platform mandates a different reset value for some PMP registers’ A and L fields.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: If the hypervisor extension is implemented, the `hgatp`.MODE and `vsatp`.MODE fields are reset to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: If the Smrnmi extension is implemented, the `mnstatus`.NMIE field is reset to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: No *WARL* field contains an illegal value.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: If the Zicfilp extension is implemented, the `mseccfg`.MLPE field is reset to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: All other hart state is UNSPECIFIED.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: The `MML`, `MMWP`, and `RLB` fields of the `mseccfg` register are set to 0, unless the platform mandates a different reset value.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: The `mcause` values after reset have implementation-specific interpretation, but the value 0 should be returned on implementations that do not distinguish different reset conditions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: Implementations that distinguish different reset conditions should only use 0 to indicate the most complete reset.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: The `USEED` and `SSEED` fields of the `mseccfg` CSR must have defined reset values.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: The system must not allow them to be in an undefined state after reset.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Reset

sentences.text: Non-maskable interrupts (NMIs) are only used for hardware error conditions, and cause an immediate jump to an implementation-defined NMI vector running in M-mode regardless of the state of a hart’s interrupt enable bits.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Non-Maskable Interrupts

sentences.text: The `mepc` register is written with the virtual address of the instruction that was interrupted, and `mcause` is set to a value indicating the source of the NMI.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Non-Maskable Interrupts

sentences.text: The NMI can thus overwrite state in an active machine-mode interrupt handler.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Non-Maskable Interrupts

sentences.text: The values written to `mcause` on an NMI are implementation-defined.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Non-Maskable Interrupts

sentences.text: The high Interrupt bit of `mcause` should be set to indicate that this was an interrupt.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Non-Maskable Interrupts

sentences.text: An Exception Code of 0 is reserved to mean "unknown cause" and implementations that do not distinguish sources of NMIs via the `mcause` register should return 0 in the Exception Code.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Non-Maskable Interrupts

sentences.text: Unlike resets, NMIs do not reset processor state, enabling diagnosis, reporting, and possible containment of the hardware error.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Non-Maskable Interrupts

sentences.text: The physical memory map for a complete system includes various address ranges, some corresponding to memory regions and some to memory-mapped control registers, portions of which might not be accessible.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Some memory regions might not support reads, writes, or execution; some might not support subword or subblock accesses; some might not support atomic operations; and some might not support cache coherence or might have different memory models.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Similarly, memory-mapped control registers vary in their supported access widths, support for atomic operations, and whether read and write accesses have associated side effects.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: In RISC-V systems, these properties and capabilities of each region of the machine's physical address space are termed physical memory attributes (PMAs).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: This section describes RISC-V PMA terminology and how RISC-V systems implement and check PMAs.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: PMAs are inherent properties of the underlying hardware and rarely change during system operation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Unlike physical memory protection values described in , PMAs do not vary by execution context.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: The PMAs of some memory regions are fixed at chip design time—for example, for an on-chip ROM.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Others are fixed at board design time, depending, for example, on which other chips are connected to off-chip buses.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Off-chip buses might also support devices that could be changed on every power cycle (cold pluggable) or dynamically while the system is running (hot pluggable).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Some devices might be configurable at run time to support different uses that imply different PMAs—for example, an on-chip scratchpad RAM might be cached privately by one core in one end-application, or accessed as a shared non-cached memory in another end-application.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Most systems will require that at least some PMAs are dynamically checked in hardware later in the execution pipeline after the physical address is known, as some operations will not be supported at all physical memory addresses, and some operations require knowing the current setting of a configurable PMA attribute.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: While many other architectures specify some PMAs in the virtual memory page tables and use the TLB to inform the pipeline of these properties, this approach injects platform-specific information into a virtualized layer and can cause system errors unless attributes are correctly initialized in each page-table entry for each physical memory region.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: In addition, the available page sizes might not be optimal for specifying attributes in the physical memory space, leading to address-space fragmentation and inefficient use of expensive TLB entries.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: For RISC-V, we separate out specification and checking of PMAs into a separate hardware structure, the PMA checker.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: In many cases, the attributes are known at system design time for each physical address region, and can be hardwired into the PMA checker.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Where the attributes are run-time configurable, platform-specific memory-mapped control registers can be provided to specify these attributes at a granularity appropriate to each region on the platform (e.g., for an on-chip SRAM that can be flexibly divided between cacheable and uncacheable uses). PMAs are checked for any access to physical memory, including accesses that have undergone virtual to physical memory translation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: To aid in system debugging, we strongly recommend that, where possible, RISC-V processors precisely trap physical memory accesses that fail PMA checks.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Precisely trapped PMA violations manifest as instruction, load, or store access-fault exceptions, distinct from virtual-memory page-fault exceptions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Precise PMA traps might not always be possible, for example, when probing a legacy bus architecture that uses access failures as part of the discovery mechanism.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: In this case, error responses from peripheral devices will be reported as imprecise bus-error interrupts.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: PMAs must also be readable by software to correctly access certain devices or to correctly configure other hardware components that access memory, such as DMA engines.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: As PMAs are tightly tied to a given physical platform’s organization, many details are inherently platform-specific, as is the means by which software can learn the PMA values for a platform.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Some devices, particularly legacy buses, do not support discovery of PMAs and so will give error responses or time out if an unsupported access is attempted.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Typically, platform-specific machine-mode code will extract PMAs and ultimately present this information to higher-level less-privileged software using some standard representation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: Where platforms support dynamic reconfiguration of PMAs, an interface will be provided to set the attributes by passing requests to a machine-mode driver that can correctly reconfigure the platform.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: For example, switching cacheability attributes on some memory regions might involve platform-specific operations, such as cache flushes, that are available only to machine-mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes

sentences.text: The most important characterization of a given memory address range is whether it holds regular main memory or I/O devices.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Main Memory versus I/O Regions

sentences.text: Regular main memory is required to have a number of properties, specified below, whereas I/O devices can have a much broader range of attributes.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Main Memory versus I/O Regions

sentences.text: Memory regions that do not fit into regular main memory, for example, device scratchpad RAMs, are categorized as I/O regions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Main Memory versus I/O Regions

sentences.text: NOTE: What previous versions of this specification termed vacant regions are no longer a distinct category; they are now described as I/O regions that are not accessible (i.e. lacking read, write, and execute permissions).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Main Memory versus I/O Regions

sentences.text: Main memory regions that are not accessible are also allowed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Main Memory versus I/O Regions

sentences.text: Access types specify which access widths, from 8-bit byte to long multi-word burst, are supported, and also whether misaligned accesses are supported for each access width.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Supported Access Type PMAs

sentences.text: Main memory regions always support read and write of all access widths required by the attached devices, and can specify whether instruction fetch is supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Supported Access Type PMAs

sentences.text: I/O regions can specify which combinations of read, write, or execute accesses to which data widths are supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Supported Access Type PMAs

sentences.text: For systems with page-based virtual memory, I/O and memory regions can specify which combinations of hardware page-table reads and hardware page-table writes are supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Supported Access Type PMAs

sentences.text: Atomicity PMAs describes which atomic instructions are supported in this address region.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs

sentences.text: Support for atomic instructions is divided into two categories: LR/SC and AMOs.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs

sentences.text: Within AMOs, there are four levels of support: AMONone, AMOSwap, AMOLogical, and AMOArithmetic. AMONone indicates that no AMO operations are supported. AMOSwap indicates that only `amoswap` instructions are supported in this address range. AMOLogical indicates that swap instructions plus all the logical AMOs (`amoand`, `amoor`, `amoxor`) are supported. AMOArithmetic indicates that all RISC-V AMOs defined by the A extension are supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

sentences.text: For each level of support, naturally aligned AMOs of a given width are supported if the underlying memory region supports reads and writes of that width.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

sentences.text: Main memory and I/O regions may only support a subset or none of the processor-supported atomic operations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

sentences.text: .Classes of AMOs supported by I/O regions. [i%autowidth,float="center",align="center",cols="<,<",options="header"] | |AMO Class |Supported Operations |AMONone + AMOSwap + AMOLogical + AMOArithmetic |None + `amoswap` + above + `amoand`, `amoor`, `amoxor` + above + `amoadd`, `amomin`, `amomax`, `amominu`, `amomaxu` |
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

sentences.text: The Zacas extension defines three additional levels of support: AMOCASW, AMOCASD, and AMOCASQ.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

sentences.text: AMOCASW indicates that in addition to instructions indicated by AMOArithmetic level support, the `AMOCAS.W` instruction is supported. AMOCASD indicates that in addition to instructions indicated by AMOCASW level support, the `AMOCAS.D` instruction is supported. AMOCASQ indicates that in addition to instructions indicated by AMOCASD level support, the `AMOCAS.Q` instruction is supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

sentences.text: The AMOs specified by the Zabha extension require the same level of support as the corresponding instructions in the Zaamo standard extension or the Zacas extension.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

sentences.text: The Ziccamoc extension requires AMOCASQ level support to main memory regions with both the coherence and cacheability PMAs.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

sentences.text: For LR/SC, there are three levels of support indicating combinations of the reservability and eventuality properties: RsrvNone, RsrvNonEventual, and RsrvEventual.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > Reservability PMA

sentences.text: RsrvNone indicates that no LR/SC operations are supported (the location is non-reservable).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > Reservability PMA

sentences.text: RsrvNonEventual indicates that the operations are supported (the location is reservable), but without the eventual success guarantee described in the unprivileged ISA specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > Reservability PMA

sentences.text: RsrvEventual indicates that the operations are supported and provide the eventual success guarantee.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > Reservability PMA

sentences.text: The misaligned atomicity granule PMA provides constrained support for misaligned AMOs.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: This PMA, if present, specifies the size of a misaligned atomicity granule, a naturally aligned power-of-two number of bytes.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: Specific supported values for this PMA are represented by MAGNN, e.g., MAG16 indicates the misaligned atomicity granule is at least 16 bytes.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: The misaligned atomicity granule PMA applies only to AMOs, loads and stores defined in the base ISAs, and loads and stores of no more than XLEN bits defined in the F, D, and Q extensions, and compressed encodings thereof.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: For an instruction in that set, if all accessed bytes lie within the same misaligned atomicity granule, the instruction will not raise an exception for reasons of address alignment, and the instruction will give rise to only one memory operation for the purposes of RVWMO--i.e., it will execute atomically.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: If a misaligned AMO accesses a region that does not specify a misaligned atomicity granule PMA, or if not all accessed bytes lie within the same misaligned atomicity granule, then an exception is raised.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: For regular loads and stores that access such a region or for which not all accessed bytes lie within the same atomicity granule, then either an exception is raised, or the access proceeds but is not guaranteed to be atomic.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: Implementations may raise access-fault exceptions instead of address-misaligned exceptions for some misaligned accesses, indicating the instruction should not be emulated by a trap handler.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: NOTE: LR/SC instructions are unaffected by this PMA and so always raise an exception when misaligned.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: Vector memory accesses are also unaffected, so might execute non-atomically even when contained within a misaligned atomicity granule.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: Implicit accesses are similarly unaffected by this PMA.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: The Zama16b extension requires MAG16 support to main memory regions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

sentences.text: Regions of the address space are classified as either main memory or I/O for the purposes of ordering by the FENCE instruction and atomic-instruction ordering bits.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Accesses by one hart to main memory regions are observable not only by other harts but also by other devices with the capability to initiate requests in the main memory system (e.g., DMA engines).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Coherent main memory regions always have either the RVWMO or RVTSO memory model.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Incoherent main memory regions have an implementation-defined memory model.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Accesses by one hart to an I/O region are observable not only by other harts and bus mastering devices but also by the targeted I/O devices, and I/O regions may be accessed with either relaxed or strong ordering.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Accesses to an I/O region with relaxed ordering are generally observed by other harts and bus mastering devices in a manner similar to the ordering of accesses to an RVWMO memory region, as discussed in the I/O Ordering section in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: By contrast, accesses to an I/O region with strong ordering are generally observed by other harts and bus mastering devices in program order.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Each strongly ordered I/O region specifies a numbered ordering channel, which is a mechanism by which ordering guarantees can be provided between different I/O regions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Channel 0 is used to indicate point-to-point strong ordering only, where only accesses by the hart to the single associated I/O region are strongly ordered.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Channel 1 is used to provide global strong ordering across all I/O regions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Any accesses by a hart to any I/O region associated with channel 1 can only be observed to have occurred in program order by all other harts and I/O devices, including relative to accesses made by that hart to relaxed I/O regions or strongly ordered I/O regions with different channel numbers.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: In other words, any access to a region in channel 1 is equivalent to executing a `fence io,io` instruction before and after the instruction.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Other larger channel numbers provide program ordering to accesses by that hart across any regions with the same channel number
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Systems might support dynamic configuration of ordering properties on each memory region.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

sentences.text: Coherence is a property defined for a single physical address, and indicates that writes to that address by one agent will eventually be made visible to other coherent agents in the system.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

sentences.text: Coherence is not to be confused with the memory consistency model of a system, which defines what values a memory read can return given the previous history of reads and writes to the entire memory system.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

sentences.text: In RISC-V platforms, the use of hardware-incoherent regions is discouraged due to software complexity, performance, and energy impacts.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

sentences.text: The cacheability of a memory region should not affect the software view of the region except for differences reflected in other PMAs, such as main memory versus I/O classification, memory ordering, supported accesses and atomic operations, and coherence.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

sentences.text: For this reason, we treat cacheability as a platform-level setting managed by machine-mode software only.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

sentences.text: Where a platform supports configurable cacheability settings for a memory region, a platform-specific machine-mode routine will change the settings and flush caches if necessary, so the system is only incoherent during the transition between cacheability settings.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

sentences.text: This transitory state should not be visible to lower privilege levels.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

sentences.text: If a PMA indicates non-cacheability, then accesses to that region must be satisfied by the memory itself, not by any caches.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

sentences.text: Idempotency PMAs describe whether reads and writes to an address region are idempotent.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: Main memory regions are assumed to be idempotent.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: For I/O regions, idempotency on reads and writes can be specified separately (e.g., reads are idempotent but writes are not).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: If accesses are non-idempotent, i.e., there is potentially a side effect on any read or write access, then speculative or redundant accesses must be avoided.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: For the purposes of defining the idempotency PMAs, changes in observed memory ordering created by redundant accesses are not considered a side effect.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: For non-idempotent regions, implicit reads and writes must not be performed early or speculatively, with the following exceptions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: When a non-speculative implicit read is performed, an implementation is permitted to additionally read any of the bytes within a naturally aligned power-of-2 region containing the address of the non-speculative implicit read.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: Furthermore, when a non-speculative instruction fetch is performed, an implementation is permitted to additionally read any of the bytes within the next naturally aligned power-of-2 region of the same size (with the address of the region taken modulo 2^XLEN^).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: The results of these additional reads may be used to satisfy subsequent early or speculative implicit reads.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: The size of these naturally aligned power-of-2 regions is implementation-defined, but, for systems with page-based virtual memory, must not exceed the smallest supported page size.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

sentences.text: To support secure processing and contain faults, it is desirable to limit the physical addresses accessible by software running on a hart.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

sentences.text: An optional physical memory protection (PMP) unit provides per-hart machine-mode control registers to allow physical memory access privileges (read, write, execute) to be specified for each physical memory region.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

sentences.text: The PMP values are checked in parallel with the PMA checks described in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

sentences.text: The granularity of PMP access control settings are platform-specific, but the standard PMP encoding supports regions as small as four bytes.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

sentences.text: Certain regions’ privileges can be hardwired—for example, some regions might only ever be visible in machine mode but in no lower-privilege layers.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

sentences.text: PMP checks are applied to all accesses whose effective privilege mode is S or U, including instruction fetches and data accesses in S and U mode, and data accesses in M-mode when the MPRV bit in `mstatus` is set and the MPP field in `mstatus` contains S or U. PMP checks are also applied to page-table accesses for virtual-address translation, for which the effective privilege mode is S.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

sentences.text: Optionally, PMP checks may additionally apply to M-mode accesses, in which case the PMP registers themselves are locked, so that even M-mode software cannot change them until the hart is reset.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

sentences.text: In effect, PMP can grant permissions to S and U modes, which by default have none, and can revoke permissions from M-mode, which by default has full permissions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

sentences.text: PMP violations are always trapped precisely at the processor.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

sentences.text: PMP entries are described by an 8-bit configuration register and one MXLEN-bit address register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: Some PMP settings additionally use the address register associated with the preceding PMP entry.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: Up to 64 PMP entries are supported.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: Implementations may implement zero, 16, or 64 PMP entries; the lowest-numbered PMP entries must be implemented first.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: All PMP CSR fields are *WARL* and may be read-only zero. PMP CSRs are only accessible to M-mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: The PMP configuration registers are densely packed into CSRs to minimize context-switch time.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: For RV32, sixteen CSRs, `pmpcfg0`–`pmpcfg15`, hold the configurations `pmp0cfg`–`pmp63cfg` for the 64 PMP entries, as shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: For RV64, eight even-numbered CSRs, `pmpcfg0`, `pmpcfg2`, …, `pmpcfg14`, hold the configurations for the 64 PMP entries, as shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: For RV64, the odd-numbered configuration registers, `pmpcfg1`, `pmpcfg3`, …, `pmpcfg15`, are illegal.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: .RV32 PMP configuration CSR layout. include::images/bytefield/pmp-rv32.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: .RV64 PMP configuration CSR layout. include::images/bytefield/pmp-rv64.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: The PMP address registers are CSRs named `pmpaddr0`-`pmpaddr63`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: Each PMP address register encodes bits 33-2 of a 34-bit physical address for RV32, as shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: For RV64, each PMP address register encodes bits 55-2 of a 56-bit physical address, as shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: Not all physical address bits may be implemented, and so the `pmpaddr` registers are *WARL*.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: .PMP address register format, RV32. include::images/bytefield/pmpaddr-rv32.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: .PMP address register format, RV64. include::images/bytefield/pmpaddr-rv64.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: shows the layout of a PMP configuration register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: The R, W, and X bits, when set, indicate that the PMP entry permits read, write, and instruction execution, respectively.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: When one of these bits is clear, the corresponding access type is denied.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: The R, W, and X fields form a collective *WARL* field for which the combinations with R=0 and W=1 are reserved.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: The remaining two fields, A and L, are described in the following sections.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: .PMP configuration register format. include::images/bytefield/pmpcfg.edn[]
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: Attempting to fetch an instruction from a PMP region that does not have execute permissions raises an instruction access-fault exception.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: Attempting to execute a load, load-reserved, or cache-block management instruction which accesses a physical address within a PMP region without read permissions raises a load access-fault exception.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: Attempting to execute a store, store-conditional, AMO, or cache-block zero instruction which accesses a physical address within a PMP region without write permissions raises a store access-fault exception.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

sentences.text: The A field in a PMP entry's configuration register encodes the address-matching mode of the associated PMP address register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: The encoding of this field is shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: When A=0, this PMP entry is disabled and matches no addresses.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: Two other address-matching modes are supported: naturally aligned power-of-2 regions (NAPOT), including the special case of naturally aligned four-byte regions (NA4); and the top boundary of an arbitrary range (TOR).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: These modes support four-byte granularity.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: .Encoding of A field in PMP configuration registers. | |A |Name |Description |0 + 1 + 2 + 3 |OFF + TOR + NA4 + NAPOT |Null region (disabled) + Top of range + Naturally aligned four-byte region + Naturally aligned power-of-two region, {ge}8 bytes |
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: NAPOT ranges make use of the low-order bits of the associated address register to encode the size of the range, as shown in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: .`NAPOT` range encoding in PMP address and configuration registers. | |`pmpaddr` |`pmpcfg`.A |Match type and size |`yyyy...yyyy` + `yyyy...yyy0` + `yyyy...yy01` + `yyyy...y011` + ... + `yy01...1111` + `y011...1111` + `0111...1111` + `1111...1111` |NA4 + NAPOT + NAPOT + NAPOT + ... + NAPOT + NAPOT + NAPOT + NAPOT |4-byte NAPOT range + 8-byte NAPOT range + 16-byte NAPOT range + 32-byte NAPOT range + … + 2^XLEN^-byte NAPOT range + 2^XLEN+1^-byte NAPOT range + 2^XLEN+2^-byte NAPOT range + 2^XLEN+3^-byte NAPOT range |
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: If TOR is selected, the associated address register forms the top of the address range, and the preceding PMP address register forms the bottom of the address range.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: If PMP entry i's A field is set to TOR, the entry matches any address y such that `pmpaddr~i-1~`{le}y<``pmpaddr~i~`` (irrespective of the value of `pmpcfg~i-1~`).
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: If PMP entry 0's A field is set to TOR, zero is used for the lower bound, and so it matches any address `y<pmpaddr~0~`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: Although the PMP mechanism supports regions as small as four bytes, platforms may specify coarser PMP regions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: In general, the PMP grain is 2^G+2^ bytes and must be the same across all PMP regions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: When G {ge} 1, the NA4 mode is not selectable.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: When G {ge} 2 and pmpcfg~i~.A[1] is set, i.e. the mode is NAPOT, then bits pmpaddr~i~[G-2:0] read as all ones.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: When G {ge} 1 and pmpcfg~i~.A[1] is clear, i.e. the mode is OFF or TOR, then bits pmpaddr~i~[G-1:0] read as all zeros.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: Bits pmpaddr~i~[G-1:0] do not affect the TOR address-matching logic.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: Although changing pmpcfg~i~.A[1] affects the value read from pmpaddr~i~, it does not affect the underlying value stored in that register—in particular, pmpaddr~i~[G-1] retains its original value when pmpcfg~i~.A is changed from NAPOT to TOR/OFF then back to NAPOT.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

sentences.text: The L bit indicates that the PMP entry is locked, i.e., writes to the configuration register and associated address registers are ignored.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

sentences.text: Locked PMP entries remain locked until the hart is reset.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

sentences.text: If PMP entry i is locked, writes to ``pmp``i``cfg`` and ``pmpaddr``i are ignored.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

sentences.text: Additionally, if PMP entry i is locked and ``pmp``i``cfg.A`` is set to TOR, writes to ``pmpaddr``i-1 are ignored.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

sentences.text: In addition to locking the PMP entry, the L bit indicates whether the R/W/X permissions are additionally enforced on M-mode accesses.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

sentences.text: When the L bit is set, these permissions are enforced for all privilege modes.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

sentences.text: When the L bit is clear, any M-mode access matching the PMP entry will succeed; the R/W/X permissions apply only to S and U modes.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

sentences.text: On some implementations, misaligned loads, stores, and instruction fetches may be decomposed into multiple memory operations, some of which may succeed before an access-fault exception occurs, as described in the RVWMO specification. PMP checking is performed on each memory operation independently.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: In particular, a portion of a misaligned store that passes the PMP check may become visible, even if another portion fails the PMP check.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: The same behavior may manifest for stores wider than XLEN bits (e.g., the FSD instruction in RV32D), even when the store address is naturally aligned.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: PMP entries are statically prioritized.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: The lowest-numbered PMP entry that matches any byte of a memory operation determines whether that operation succeeds or fails.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: The matching PMP entry must match all bytes of a memory operation, or the operation fails, irrespective of the L, R, W, and X bits.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: For example, if a PMP entry is configured to match the four-byte range `0xC`–`0xF`, then an 8-byte access to the range `0x8`–`0xF` will fail, assuming that PMP entry is the highest-priority entry that matches those addresses.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: If a PMP entry matches all bytes of a memory operation, then the L, R, W, and X bits determine whether the operation succeeds or fails.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: If the L bit is clear and the privilege mode of the access is M, the operation succeeds.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: Otherwise, if the L bit is set or the privilege mode of the access is S or U, then the operation succeeds only if the R, W, or X bit corresponding to the access type is set.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: If no PMP entry matches an M-mode memory operation, the operation succeeds.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: If no PMP entry matches an S-mode or U-mode memory operation, but at least one PMP entry is implemented, the operation fails.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: Failed memory operations generate an instruction, load, or store access-fault exception.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: Note that a single instruction may generate multiple memory operations, which may not be mutually atomic.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: An access-fault exception is generated if at least one memory operation generated by an instruction fails, though other memory operations generated by that instruction may succeed with visible side effects.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: Notably, instructions that reference virtual memory are decomposed into multiple memory operations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

sentences.text: The Physical Memory Protection mechanism is designed to compose with the page-based virtual memory systems described in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

sentences.text: When paging is enabled, instructions that access virtual memory may result in multiple physical-memory accesses, including implicit references to the page tables.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

sentences.text: The PMP checks apply to all of these accesses.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

sentences.text: The effective privilege mode for implicit page-table accesses is S.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

sentences.text: Implementations with virtual memory are permitted to perform address translations speculatively and earlier than required by an explicit memory access, and are permitted to cache them in address translation cache structures—including possibly caching the identity mappings from effective address to physical address used in Bare translation modes and M-mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

sentences.text: The PMP settings for the resulting physical address may be checked (and possibly cached) at any point between the address translation and the explicit memory access.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

sentences.text: Hence, when the PMP settings are modified, M-mode software must synchronize the PMP settings with the virtual memory system and any PMP or address-translation caches.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

sentences.text: This is accomplished by executing an SFENCE.VMA instruction with rs1=`x0` and rs2=`x0`, after the PMP CSRs are written.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

sentences.text: See for additional synchronization requirements when the hypervisor extension is implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

sentences.text: If page-based virtual memory is not implemented, memory accesses check the PMP settings synchronously, so no SFENCE.VMA is needed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging
